0.response: {"Q0":"I focused on getting blue and red gyms, and ignoring the yellow planet. I only realized FAR TO LATE that the green ice can appear on yellow planets. I did the math. Grabbing green ice in a meteor shower is only worth it if 2/3 chance of gems can be sold. Once one of the astronauts left, it became a 10gp expense for a 2/3 failure at 20 gp. So, I realized Ice is random, and for the cost of 3gp to mine it, it was lucrative to get it on red and blue planets, even if the astronaut left since it is random. I gave a shot on a yellow planet and realized during waves where everything was Ice, it was not worth it to mine there. I then discovered there are waves that switch between ice, and gems. So I focused on only red gems when the blue astronaut left, and then focused on all 3 planets during Green Ice streaks to maximize my value."}

7.response: {"Q0":"When it came to the money, I mostly focused on the highest yellow bar. Comparing the value of money, if it was over twice the amount."}

9.response: {"Q0":"Whenever a planet would switch from gems, to Green Ice."}

1.response: {"Q0":"Yes. I focused grabbing green ice from every planet once I realized yellow planets have green ice."}

2.response: {"Q0":"probably every 1/3 was a gem and 2/3 were rocks. But the investment of 9gp for a 30gp sale made it worth it."}

6.response: {"Q0":"No. I only collected gems from the astronaut who had remained. 10gp on a blue gem was a waste, and 10gp for a 2/3rd chance of 20gp was not worth it."}

8.response: {"Q0":"Planets was my primary focus, since they got me gold IN THE MOMENT."}

3.response: {"Q0":"Yes. I paid attention. But when Green Ice game in, I discovered a gold mine."}

4.response: {"Q0":"Yes. It made sense blue gems would be worthless. But having an indication when they would all be Green Ice would be valuable. I thought it was all random, until I realized some galaxies ONLY had green ice."}

11.response: {"Q0":"Some music, or something light to play in the back ground would help with the dead silence of mining in the harsh cold vastness of space."}
